Facts.text: King James I of Aragon censors Hebrew writing
Aenea Silvio Piccolomini chosen as Pope Pius II
Battle of Knockdoe, the bloodiest battle of medieval Ireland, is fought in Galway between two Anglo-Irish lords: Gearoid Fitzgerald, Lord Deputy, defeats Ulick Finn Burke

Dutch ship Liefde reaches Japan, barely making it across the Pacific with 25 crew members, including English navigatorWilliam Adams; its cannons later play an important role in the Battle of Sekigahara

World's "most mysterious book", a codex known as the Voynich manuscript, written in an unknown script, is sent by a rector of Prague University to a Jesuit scholar (now in Yale University Library and still undeciphered) in 1666
Battle of Slankamen: "The bloodiest battle of the century" Austrian Habsburg forces defeat the Ottoman army, killing Grand Vizier Köprülü Fazıl Mustafa Pasha
Battle of Santa Marta, Venezuela: English fleet defeats the French (battle ends August 25)
Battle of Gross-Jägersdorf: Russian army defeats Prussia [New style = August 30]
Empress Catherine II commissions a new version of the Saint Isaac's Cathedral in St. Petersburg, Russia
Yellow fever epidemic in Philadelphia, then the US capital, has its first fatality and lasts until November, killing around 5,000 people
Spain and France sign an anti-British alliance
US warship Constitution defeats British warship Guerriere
Gervasio Antonio de Posadas joins Argentina's second triumvirate
Java returns to Dutch rule after being controlled by the British for five years
Canada Company is chartered to colonize Upper Canada (Ontario)
HMS Beagle anchors at Angra, Azores
Confederate Congress allies with the government of Missouri
Second day of battle at Globe Tavern, Virginia, Union forces attempt to destroy Weldon Railroad Confederate supply route (successful August 21)
William Huggins describes the astronomical application of spectroscopy
American frontier murderer and outlaw John Wesley Hardin is shot and killed by John Selman Sr. in a saloon in El Paso, Texas
First electric taxis drive in London
Start of the first and only Olympic cricket match as Great Britain defeats France by 158 runs in Paris
Philadelphia Phillies suffer a record ninth consecutive postponed game

"Wild" Bob Burman wins his first major auto race at Indianapolis Motor Speedway, earning the Prest-O-Lite Trophy; the 100-lap, 250-mile race is completed by only three of the nine cars, and one racer and a mechanic are killed in a crash
Percy Grainger's orchestral piece "Shepherd's Hey" premieres
Frenchman Adolphe Célestin Pégoud makes the first parachute jump in Europe
German army executes 150 Belgians by firing squad
German fleet bombs the English coast
Harris Theatre (Candler, Coan & Harris) opens at 226 W 42nd St, New York City
British liner "SS Arabic" is sunk by a German submarine without warning while leaving Liverpool for New York, killing 44 and creating a diplomatic incident
Rationing laws go into effect in the Netherlands
Sunday benefit baseball game at NYC's Polo Grounds results in John McGraw andChristy Mathewson's arrest for violating Blue Laws
After nearly 100 years of British control, Afghanistan declares independence
Metropolitan Sergius proclaims the declaration of loyalty of the Russian Orthodox Church to the Soviet state
Lefty Grove wins an AL record-tying 16th consecutive game
The first All-American Soap Box Derby is held in Dayton, Ohio
Trial against Lev Kamenev and Grigory Zinoviev for "Trotskyism" opens in Moscow
37.6 cm of rainfall in Tuckerton, NJ (state record)
World War II: Over 4,000 Canadian and British soldiers are killed, wounded, or captured during a raid on Dieppe, France
Belgian church excommunicates Nazi collaborator Léon Degrelle
US air raid on German bases at Gilze-Rijen and Vlissingen, the Netherlands
Allied air raid on Maastricht kills more than 80 people
Last Japanese troops driven out of India
Nazis give parts of Paris to the Resistance
Paris police strike against Nazi occupiers
Polish 1st Division occupies Hill 262 (Mont Ormel), Normandy
US 15th Army Corps occupies Mantes-Gassicourt near Paris
US 90th and Polish 1st Divisions occupy Chambois, Normandy
J. Arens and D. van Dorpen synthesize vitamin A
ABC begins broadcasting Saturday morning kids' shows (Animal Clinic and Acrobat Ranch)
32.4 cm of rainfall in Burlington, Connecticut (state record)
Hurricane Diane kills 200 people and becomes the first billion-dollar damage storm in the northeastern United States
US raises import duty on bicycles by 50%
NY Giants board of directors vote 8-1 to move their baseball franchise to San Francisco in 1958
US Major David Simons reaches 30,933 meters in a balloon
NAACP Youth Council begins a sit-in at a "whites-only" lunch counter at Katz Drug Store in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma
Honolulu, Hawaii seeks a franchise in baseball's new Continental League
Satellite Discoverer 6 is launched into polar orbit
New England's first commercial atomic energy reactor, owned by the Yankee Atomic Electric Company, achieves a self-sustaining nuclear reaction in Rowe, Massachusetts
Soviet Sputnik 5 carries 2 dogs, 2 rats, 40 mice, 1 rabbit, and fruit flies into space, becoming the first animals to return alive from orbit
Homer Blancos plays his finest round in golf, shooting a 55
Communication satellite Syncom 3 is launched
Auschwitz trials end with six life sentences
Cincinnati Reds pitcher Jim Maloney throws his second no-hitter of the season in a 1-0 win over the Chicago Cubs
The Yardbirds launch their first US tour
Earthquake strikes Varto region in eastern Turkey with a 6.8 magnitude, killing around 2,400
The Beatles are pelted with rotten fruit and firecrackers during a concert at the Mid-South Coliseum in Memphis, Tennessee; the Ku Klux Klan demonstrates outside the show and burns records
The Beatles' "All You Need Is Love" single goes to #1
The Chinese community in South Africa is granted "white" status
France performs a nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
USSR performs a nuclear test at Sary Shagan, USSR
Between 377 and 470 people die in an arson fire at a movie theater in Abadan, Iran
"My Sharona" by The Knack hits #1 and stays at the top for 6 weeks
Soviet Cosmonauts Vladimir Lyakhov and Valery Ryumin return to Earth aboard Soyuz 34 after a record 175 days in space
Saudi Arabian Lockheed Tristar crashes on landing at Riyadh, killing 301
Willy Russell's play "Educating Rita" premieres in London, starring Julie Walters
Renaldo Nehemiah of the US runs the 110 m hurdles in a world record time of 12.93 seconds
Two US Navy F-14 jet fighters shoot down two Soviet-built Libyan SU-22s
Soyuz T-7 launches; Svetlana Savitskaya becomes the second woman in space
Dodgers trade Dave Stewart and Ricky Wright to Texas for Rick Honeycutt
Japan launches Suisei, the country's second probe of Halley's Comet
Car bomb kills 20 in Tehran, Iran
Hungerford Massacre, England: Michael Ryan kills 16 people with an assault rifle and then commits suicide
Iran and Iraq begin a ceasefire in their eight-year-old war at 11 pm EDT
Maung Maung succeeds General Sein Lwin as the 7th President of Burma
First crack in the Iron Curtain as Hungary opens its borders to Austria for a Pan-European Picnic for a few hours
Tadeusz Mazowiecki is elected as the first non-communist Prime Minister of Poland
Dodger José Offerman hits a home run in his first at-bat
Hurricane Bob hits the US, making landfall in Massachusetts
Sri Lanka makes its highest cricket score ever, 8-547, against Australia
Sri Lankan cricket wicketkeeper Romesh Kaluwitharana scores 132 on debut in the first Test against Australia in Colombo
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits record high of 3612.13
George Tiller, an abortion doctor, is shot in his arms by Rachelle Shannon
Mattel and Fisher-Price toy companies merge
Sally Gunnell runs a women's world record 400 m hurdles (52.74 seconds)
After five days, Shannon Faulkner quits as the first woman at The Citadel
Bruce Seldon TKO's Joe Hipp in the 10th round for the heavyweight boxing title
Major South African political parties begin their submissions to the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC)
STS-85 (Discovery 23) lands

South African Truth and Reconciliation Commission chairperson, ArchbishopDesmond Tutu, releases documents revealing an alleged plot by Western countries to assassinate UN Secretary-General Dag Hammarskjöld of Sweden
A Russian Mi-26 helicopter carrying troops is hit by a Chechen missile outside Grozny, killing 127 soldiers and injuring 20 in the worst attack in helicopter history
A car bomb attack on United Nations headquarters in Iraq kills the agency's top envoy, Sergio Vieira de Mello, and 21 other employees
A Hamas-planned suicide attack on a bus in Jerusalem kills 23 Israelis, 7 of whom are children
Australian swimmer Jodie Henry records 53.84 to win the women's 100m freestyle gold medal ahead of Dutch star Inge de Bruijn at the Athens Olympics
US gymnast Carly Patterson becomes the second American woman to win the all-around gold medal and the first to win at a non-boycotted Olympic Games when she takes gold in Athens

A series of strong storms dubbed the "Toronto Supercell" lashes Southern Ontario, spawning several tornadoes and creating extreme flash flooding within the city of Toronto and its surrounding communities
The first-ever joint military exercise between Russia and China, called Peace Mission 2005 begins
15.12 inches (38.4 cm) of rainfall in Hokah, Minnesota (a state record, breaking the previous record after 35 years)
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, Crosswater Club: Mark McNulty of Zimbabwe wins lone career major title by 5 strokes from David Edwards
Britain's Victoria Pendleton beats Anna Mears in the women's cycling sprint final at the Beijing Olympics, with Great Britain dominating the track by winning 7 of 10 gold medals
Chris Hoy encapsulates British dominance in track cycling at the Beijing Olympics by beating teammate Jason Kenny to win the sprint, adding to his keirin and team sprint gold medals
Reigning world champion Christine Ohuruogu wins the women's 400 m at the Beijing Olympics, earning Great Britain's 50th gold medal in Olympic track and field competition
The men's gymnastics program at the Beijing Olympics concludes with the home team from China dominating the medals tally, taking 7 of 8 gold medals; Zou Kai wins 3
A series of bombings in Baghdad, Iraq, kill 101 and injure 565

Plane crash in the mountainous region of Talodi, Sudan, kills 32, including several government officials and staff, en route to the Eid al-Fitr festival, which celebrates the end of the Muslim holy month of Ramadan; crash is blamed on bad weather conditions
24 Egyptian policemen are killed in an attack in Rafah
37 pilgrims are killed in a train accident in India
91 people are killed by floods across China
The 24-hour ceasefire extension renewal between Israel and Palestine is violated as Hamas fires rockets; the Israeli Air Force responds, killing 9 Gazans
US Food and Drug Administration approves female Viagra libido pill Addyi
A six-way tie for first place occurs in the final of the equestrian individual jumping competition at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; Nick Skelton of Great Britain wins the gold medal jump-off

Chloe Esposito of Australia sets an Olympic record with an overall score of 1,372 points to win the women's modern pentathlon in Rio de Janeiro. Élodie Clouvel of France is second with 1,356 points, and Oktawia Nowacka of Poland is third with 1,349 points
Colombian BMX rider Mariana Pajón successfully defends her Olympic title by beating American Alise Post in the final in Rio de Janeiro
Dilshod Nazarov of Tajikistan throws 78.68 m to win the men's hammer throw gold medal at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, earning the first gold medal for Tajikistan in Olympic history
Germany defeats Sweden 2-1 in the women's football gold medal match at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, with 52,432 spectators at Maracanã Stadium

Great Britain upsets the heavily favored Netherlands to win the women's field hockey gold medal at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics after drawing 3-3 in regulation time and winning a dramatic penalty shootout 2-0
Kiley Neushul scores three goals as the US women's water polo team routs Italy 12-5 to win the gold medal in Rio de Janeiro and retain their Olympic title
Vivian Cheruiyot of Kenya goes one better than her London silver medal by winning the women's 5,000m at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, running an Olympic record time of 14:26.17
Limerick wins the All-Ireland Hurling Championship for the first time in 45 years with a 3-16 to 2-18 victory over defending champions Galway at Croke Park, Dublin
Monsoon rains finally ease in Kerala State, India, with flooding taking 350 lives and 200,000 in relief camps
N. K. Jemisin becomes the first author to win the Hugo Award for Best Novel in three consecutive years with her work "The Stone Sky"; she is also the first to win for all three novels in a trilogy
Two more earthquakes hit Lombok in Indonesia, killing 14 people two weeks after previous earthquakes
Weinstein accuser Asia Argento is alleged to have sexually assaulted a 17-year-old in an article by "The New York Times"
Evelyn Hernández is cleared of killing her newborn baby in a landmark case in a retrial in El Salvador
Apple becomes the first US company to be valued at $2 trillion just two years after it reaches a $1 trillion valuation
Golfing dinner termed #GolfGate, appearing to flout COVID-19 restrictions and attended by Irish political figures, prompts two politicians to later resign
Largest floods in 70 years wet the toes of giant 71 m Leshan Buddha carved by a river just outside Chengdu, China, with 100,000 people evacuated
US Postmaster General Louis DeJoy says he will suspend the controversial plan to cut costs until after the election
Iran's official COVID-19 death toll surpasses 100,000 amid its fifth wave of infections, according to its Health Ministry
30-hour siege begins at Hayat Hotel, Mogadishu, by Shabab militants, leaving 21 dead and 117 injured
European drought uncovers river "hunger stones" that once warn of famine, including the Děčín stone (earliest inscription 1417) on the Elbe River, with the carving "if you see me, then weep"
Mexico's former Attorney General Jesús Murillo Karam is arrested in connection with the disappearance of 43 students in 2014, one of the country's worst human rights tragedies
MLB Philadelphia Phillies shortstop Trea Turner hits two solo home runs in the same inning in a 12-3 win over the Nationals in Washington
English tech entrepreneur Mike Lynch and five others die when their luxury yacht capsizes in a storm off the coast of Palermo
Sydney opens a new multi-billion-dollar metro line between Chatswood and Sydenham, the city's biggest transport change since the opening of the Sydney Harbour Bridge
John Wall, NBA five-time All-Star and Washington Wizards No. 1 draft pick in 2010, announces his retirement after 11 seasons
Octavian, later known asAugustus, compels the Roman Senate to elect him consul
Edward Iis crowned King of England at Westminster Abbey after returning from the Ninth Crusade
KingRichard IIof England surrenders to his cousin Henry Bolingbroke at Flint Castle after promising to abdicate if his life was spared
Holy RomanEmperor Maximilian Ibecomes Archduke of Austria on the death of his father and co-ruler Frederick III
EmperorCharles V's troops besiege Marseille, France
MaryQueen of Scotsarrives in Leith, Scotland to assume the throne after spending 13 years in France
Sigismund IIIbecomes King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania
Prince of OrangeFrederick Henryconquers Fort Groenlo after a siege during the Dutch Revolt against Spain
Jacobite Rising 1745:Bonnie Prince Charlieraises his standard at Glenfinnan, Scotland, igniting the second Jacobite rebellion
KingGustav IIIseizes effective control of Swedish government and restores full power of monarchy, which had been subordinate to parliament since 1720

Battle of Blue Licks: 50 Loyalists and 300 Indigenous warriors ambush and rout 182 Kentucky militiamen, includingDaniel Boone, in Kentucky County, Virginia, in one of the last battles of the American Revolutionary War
Failed liberal coup against French KingLouis XVIII
Dmitri Mendeleevmakes a solo ascent by balloon to an altitude of 11,500 feet (3.5 km) above Klin, Russia, to observe an eclipse
Russian TsarNicholas IIinstalls the "Imperial Duma" without legislative powers
New York GiantChristy Mathewsonloses after defeating the Reds 22 consecutive times
Elmer Rice's play "On Trial" premieres in New York City
In a message to the Senate, US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonurges the American people to be "neutral in fact as well as name"
Irving Berlin's military musical revue "Yip Yip Yaphank" opens at the Century Theatre and later transfers to the Lexington Theatre in NYC
Detroit's TigerTy Cobbis the fourth and youngest player to achieve 3,000 hits against the Boston Red Sox
Baseball umpire Jocko Conlan ejects Pirates managerFrankie Frischfor coming onto the field holding an umbrella to get a rainout
Winston Churchillvisits Field Marshal Montgomery's headquarters in Burg el-Arab
World War II: GeneralFriedrich Paulusorders the German 6th Army to conquer Stalingrad
US generalOmar Bradleyvisits British generalBernard Montgomery
Phillies'Jimmie Foxx, 37, pitches the first 7 innings against the Reds and wins
Bill Veeckof the St. Louis Browns sends Eddie Gaedel, a 3 ft 7 in (1.09 m) little person, to pinch-hit; he walks on four pitches

Democratically elected Prime Minister of Iran MohammadMossadeghis overthrown in a coup orchestrated by the United Kingdom (under the name 'Operation Boot') and the United States (under the name TPAJAX Project)
AmericanRalph Buncheis named Undersecretary of the UN
WINS radio in New York City announces it will not play white cover versions of R&B records, opting instead to playFats Domino's "Ain't That A Shame" rather than Pat Boone's

American CIA pilotFrancis Gary Powersis convicted of spying by the USSR (U-2 incident) and sentenced to 3 years in prison plus 7 in a labor camp; he serves 17 months before being exchanged for a captured Soviet KGB spy
US vice-presidentLyndon B. Johnsonvisits West Berlin
Film adaptation ofArlo Guthrie's song "Alice's Restaurant," directed by Arthur Penn and starring Guthrie, premieres
MLB Chicago Cubs pitcherKen Holtzmanno-hits the visiting Atlanta Braves 3-0 at Wrigley Field

RCA Records releases "Pussy Cats," the tenth album by American singerHarry Nilssonin the US; the album is produced byJohn Lennon, and Nilsson works through a serious vocal cord injury that diminishes his acclaimed range
PresidentGerald Fordwins the Republican presidential nomination at the Kansas City convention
Kansas City Royals'George Brett's hitting streak ends at 30 games
LSU footballerBilly Cannonis sentenced to 5 years for counterfeiting
Republican convention in Dallas, Texas nominates incumbentRonald Reaganfor President

ArchbishopDesmond TutusnubsP. W. Botha's invitation to attend a meeting to discuss the role and actions of the police and security forces in South Africa following the Rubicon speech four days earlier
NY Rangers sign ex-Canadian greatGuy Lafleurout of retirement
Conservative members of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union attempt to deposeMikhail Gorbachevin a coup d'état
Mike Tysonreturns to the ring after serving 3 years in prison and defeats Peter McNeeley by disqualification in 89 seconds
NY Yankees third basemanWade Boggspitches a scoreless inning against Anaheim
In Belgrade, tens of thousands of Serbians rally to demand the resignation ofSlobodan Miloševićas president of the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia
German Ferrari driverMichael Schumacherwins the Hungarian Grand Prix at the Hungaroring to clinch his fourth F1 World Drivers' Championship and equalAlain Prost's record of 51 Grand Prix victories
American swimmerAaron Peirsolwins the men's 200 m backstroke in an Olympic record time of 1:54.95, achieving the 100/200 m backstroke double at the Athens Olympics
American swimmerMichael Phelpswraps up the 200/400 m individual medley double at the Athens Olympics as he wins the 200 m (1:57.14 OR) ahead of teammateRyan Lochte
Interscope Records releases "The Fame," the debut album byLady Gaga(Grammy Award for Best Electronic/Dance Album 2010)
City of San Francisco unveils a statue of singerTony Bennettoutside the Fairmont Hotel, where he first performs his signature song "I Left My Heart in San Francisco" in 1961
Novak ĐokovićbeatsRoger Federer6-4, 6-4 in the final of the Cincinnati Masters to become the first player to win all nine Masters 1,000 tennis tournaments since the series started in 1990
Sudanese ex-PresidentOmar al-Bashiradmits he has received $90 million from Saudi Arabian royals at the start of his corruption trial in Khartoum

Five more people—George Jacobs, Martha Carrier, George Burroughs, John Proctor, and John Willard—are hanged for allegedly practicing witchcraft as a result of the Salem witch trials in Massachusetts Bay Colony

Benjamin Bannekersends a copy of his Almanac and writes a letter to Thomas Jefferson criticizing his pro-slavery stance and requesting justice for African Americans using language from the Declaration of Independence
Louis Daguerre'sdaguerreotypephotographic process with complete working instructions is published "free to the world" in Paris as a gift from the French government
New York Heraldis the first major eastern newspaper to report the discovery of gold in California
Romania annexes the Transnistria territory from the Soviet Union following Operation Barbarossa
Operation Iraqi Freedom winds down as the last of the United States Brigade Combat Teams cross the border into Kuwait
NASA satellites take photos showing that the eastern basin of the Aral Sea has, for the first time, completely dried up
Catharine of Bohemia, Duchess of Bavaria, born in Prague, Bohemia (d. 1395)
Marqués de Santillana, Castilian politician and poet (serranillas), born in Carrión de los Condes, Spain (d. 1458)
Lodovico Guicciardini, Italian historian (Descrittione di Tutti), born in Florence (d. 1589)
Frederick I, Duke of Württemberg, born in Montbéliard, France (d. 1608)
Paulus Merula, Dutch lawyer and historian (Historia Belgica), born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1607)
Pierre Vernier, French mathematician, inventor of the vernier caliper used for taking accurate measurements, born in Ornans, France (d. 1637)
Henry Rich, 1st Earl of Holland, English soldier, born in Stratford-atte-Bow, Middlesex (d. 1649)
Elizabeth Stuart, Electress of the Palatinate, "The Winter Queen" of Bohemia and daughter of James VI and I of England and Scotland, born at Dunfermline Palace, Fife, Kingdom of Scotland (d. 1662)
Gerbrand van den Eeckhout, Dutch painter, born in Amsterdam (d. 1674)
Antonio Tonelli, Italian cellist and composer, born in Carpi, Duchy of Modena (d. 1765)
Eustace Budgell, English writer (The Spectator, writing 37 numbers signed X), born in Saint Thomas, Exeter, England (d. 1737)
Samuel Richardson, English novelist (Pamela), born in Mackworth, Derbyshire, England (d. 1761)
Edward Boscawen, British admiral in the Royal Navy, 1723-61 (Seven Years War - Battle of Lagos), and politician (Member of Parliament, 1742-61), born in Tregothnan, Cornwall, England (d. 1761)
Johann Georg Christoph Schetky, German-Scottish cellist and composer, born in Darmstadt, Landgraviate of Hesse-Darmstadt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1824)
Marie Jeanne Becu, Comtesse du Barry, last mistress of Louis XV, born in Vaucouleurs, France (d. 1793)

Samuel Prescott, American physician and patriot who with Paul Revere and William Dawes warned Massachusetts minutemen the regulars are coming before the battles of Lexington and Concord, born in Concord, Massachusetts, British America (d. 1777)[1]
Anthony van Haersolte, Dutch politician and member of Napoleonic Batavian Republic executive body, born in Zutphen, Netherlands (d. 1830)
Pierre-Jean de Béranger, French poet and songwriter, born in Paris, France (d. 1857)
Seth Thomas, American manufacturer and pioneer in the mass production of clocks, born in Wolcott, Connecticut (d. 1859)
Edward John Dent, British clockmaker to Queen Victoria and commissioned to make Big Ben, London (completed after his death by his son Frederick Dent), born in London, England (d. 1853)
James Nasmyth, Scottish engineer and inventor of the steam hammer, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1890)
Mary Ellen Pleasant, American entrepreneur, financier, real estate magnate, abolitionist, and civil rights pioneer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1904)[1]
John P. McCown, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Sevier County, Tennessee (d. 1879)
Julius van Zuylen van Nijevelt, Dutch minister of Foreign affairs, born in Dommeldange, Luxembourg (d. 1894)
Georg Goltermann, German cellist and composer, born in Hanover, Kingdom of Hanover, German Confederation (d. 1898)
Julius Lothar Meyer, German Chemist who developed the first periodic table of chemical elements independently of Dimitri Mendeleev, born in Varel Oldenburg (d. 1895)
Stephen Gano Burbridge, "Butcher of Kentucky", American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Georgetown, Kentucky (d. 1894)
Oskar Brefeld, German botanist and founder of modern mycology, born in Telgte, Germany (d. 1925)
Luis Martín, Spanish Superior-General of the Society of Jesus, born in Melgar de Fernamental, Burgos, Spain (d. 1906)
Gustave Caillebotte, French painter (Rue de Paris) and member of Impressionists, born in Paris (d. 1894)
Frans Schollaert, Belgian Catholic Party politician and 19th Prime Minister of Belgium (1908-11), born in Wilsele, Belgium (d. 1917)
Aleksei Brusilov, Russian general most noted for the development of new offensive tactics used in the 1916 Brusilov Offensive, born in Tiflis, Caucasus Viceroyalty, Russian Empire (d. 1926)
Charles Comiskey, American Baseball HOF infielder (St. Louis Brown Stockings/Browns), team owner (Chicago White Sox) and manager (St. Louis Browns), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1931)
Hippolyte Delehaye, Flemish historian and hagiographer, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1941)
John Kane, Scottish-American primitivist painter (Self-Portrait), born in West Calder, Scotland (d. 1934)
Bernard Baruch, American financier and presidential adviser (Cold War), born in Camden, South Carolina (d. 1965)
Fred Stone, renowned American circus performer and broadway actor (Alice Adams), born in Longmont, Colorado (d. 1959)
Stjepan Seljan, Croatian explorer (Ethiopia, South America), born in Karlovac, Croatia (d. 1936)
George Enescu, Romanian violinist, conductor,composer(Romanian Dances), and teacher, born in Liveni, Dorohoi, Romania (d. 1955)
Elsie Ferguson, American film actress (Footlights, Scarlet Pages), born in New York City (d. 1961)
José Mendes Cabeçadas, 95th Prime Minister of Portugal, 9th President of Portugal, helped create and end Portuguese First Republic, born in Loulé, Portugal (d. 1965)

Leonid Kulik, Russian mineralogist who led the first research expedition to study the Tunguska meteor site, the largest impact event in recorded history, born in Tartu, Estonia, Imperial Russia (d. 1942)
Werner Elert, German Lutheran theologian, born in Heldrungen, Prussia (d. 1954)
Robert Heger, German composer and conductor, born in Strasbourg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1978)
Arthur Waley, English sinologist, translator from Chinese & Japanese, born in Tunbridge Wells, England (d. 1966)
Yves Alix, French painter and graphic artist, born in Fontainebleau, France (d. 1969)
Olga Baclanova, Russian actress (Freaks, Docks of NY), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1974)
Arnoldt Bronnen, Austrian playwright, born in Vienna (d. 1959)
Eino Linnala, Finnish composer, pedagogue (University of Helsinki, 1927-66), and musicologist, born in Helsinki, Finland (d. 1973)
Walter Lang, Swiss composer, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1966)
Charlie Hall, English comedic actor (Tit for Tat, A Chump at Oxford), born in Birmingham, England (d. 1959)
Colleen Moore, American actress (The Scarlet Letter), born in Port Huron, Michigan (d. 1988)
Franz C. Schmelkes, Czech-born Chemist who discovered azochloramid (chlorazodin), widely used as a disinfectant to sterilize wounds and burns, born in Prague, Czechoslovakia (d. 1942)
Kenneth MacKenna, American actor and director (Judgment at Nuremberg, Those We Love), born in Canterbury, New Hampshire (d. 1962)
Gilbert Ryle, British philosopher, coined the phrase "ghost in the machine", born in Brighton, England (d. 1976)
J. B. L. Reyes, Filipino jurist, Associate Justice of the Supreme Court, born in Manila, Philippines (d. 1994)
Vladimir Kirshon, Russian playwright (Ultimatum Factory), born in Nalchik, Russia (d. 1938) [NS]
Claude Dauphin, French actor (Barbarella, April in Paris, Deported), born in Corbeil-Essonnes, Essonne, France (d. 1978)
James Gould Cozzens, American novelist (1949 Pulitzer-Guard of Honor), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1978)
Fitzhugh Lee, American vice-admiral (WW II - Navy Cross x2), born in Batangas, Philippines (d. 1992)
Jacques de Menasce, Austrian-American composer, born in Bad Ischl, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1960)

Eddie Durham, American pioneering jazz electric guitarist, trombonist (Count Basie; Jimmie Luceford), composer ("Topsy"; "I Don't Want to Set the World on Fire"), and bandleader (All-Star Girl Orchestra), born in San Marcos, Texas (d. 1987)
June Collyer, American actress (Before Midnight, Charley's Aunt), born in New York City (d. 1968)
Philo T. Farnsworth, American inventor (created electronic television, video camera tube), born in Beaver, Utah (d. 1971)
Jiří Srnka, Czech classical and film score composer, born in Písek, Bohemia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1982)
Thruston B. Morton, American republican politician (Sen-Ky, 1957-69), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1982)
Zygmunt Mycielski, Polish composer, music writer and editor (Ruch Muzyczny, 1957-68), born in Przeworsk, Austrian Galicia (d. 1987)[1]

(Lyle) "Spud" Murphy [Miko Stefanovic], Serbian-American jazz multi-instrumentalist, arranger (Benny Goodman; Casa Loma Orchestra), bandleader, composer (Columbia Pictures), and educator (Equal Interval System), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2005)
Al Morgan, American jazz double-bassist (Cab Calloway; Fats Waller; Louis Jordan), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1974)
Jerzy Andrzejewski, Polish writer (Ashes and Diamonds), born in Warsaw, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1983)
Quentin Bell, English art historian (established Charleston Trust), born in London, England (d. 1996)
John Argyris, Greek aeronautical engineer, one of the creators of the Finite Element Method, born in Volos, Greece (d. 2004)
Richard Simmons, American actor (Sergeant Preston of the Yukon), born in St. Paul, Minnesota (d. 2003)
Walter B. Jones Sr., American politician (Rep-D-North Carolina 1966-92), born in Fayetteville, North Carolina (d. 1992)
Fumio Hayasaka, Japanese film score composer (Rashomon; Seven Samurai), born in Sendai, Honshū, Japan (d. 1955)
Lajos Baróti, Hungarian soccer defender (2 caps; Szegedi AK, Győri ETO) and manager (Hungary 1957-66, 75-78), born in Szeged, Hungary (d. 2005)
Alfred Rouleau, French Canadian businessman (Desjardins Group), born in Sherbrooke, Quebec (d. 1985)
Ring Lardner Jr., American journalist and screenwriter (Woman of the Year, M A S H), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2000)
